feed.title: Logs
feed.subtitle: This is a collection of logs written as an open reflection.

entry.title: Relaunching The Blog
entry.content:
I’m relaunching the blog after a period of silence. The old posts will be migrated here over time. The spark came during my long paternity leave. I had time to rethink some of the things I’m doing currently. While posting has proven to be valuable tool for me to digest information so that it’s understood and remembered. The process of posting has been problematic. So I had to do changes.

I’ve been using Twitter, LinekdIn and Worpress (.com) to share my posts. To keep it short, my approach has been without a clear strategy or a plan which is asking for trouble. If you want to work an idea, it should be easy. Much like jotting notes with a pen and a notebook. I don’t want to spend time thinking which platform I should post on, how long the payload should be and how to arrange the inter-platform posting.

I somewhat enjoy Twitter since it’s simple and condensed. Fundamentally it is solid. However, for my purpose it seems too condensed. And to be honest I’m not the biggest fan of the atmosphere on that platform. Often a dishonest opinion becomes a mass reality. In addition you will get all the negatives an ad-focused social media comes with. So my approach with Twitter is the same as for protests and demonstrations: interesting to observe from the sidelines but not from where the beer bottles and riot shields clash.

LinkedIn is worse in retaining older posts. It seems like a shoot and forget type of activity which doesn’t fit the purpose of this experiment. The whole idea of a log is that it’s an organized collection of brief bits of information.

Wordpress became too much for me. I can’t commit to the workflow. It’s pure pain and not in a good way. I will be shutting it down. The tools I use must be easily accessible and simple. So while the site might work well for a wedding planner (no offense, you’re all great) it’s not for me. It’s not my goal to spend time on a prose editor and spend hours doing a simple post. The primary objective is to work and the secondary objective is to discuss the work.

This is a GitHub Pages based blog and it seems to answer my needs. The workflow is based on typical Git process which I enjoy. I get my engineering satisfaction from writing the posts on a code editor, pushing the posts over a simple SSH connection and running a local version of the blog on my laptop to preview changes before commiting them. Let alone that it supports well short and long posts. Ok, enough for now.
entry.summary: I’m relaunching the blog after a period of silence. The old posts will be migrated here over time. The spark came during my long paternity leave. I had time to rethink some of the things I’m doing currently. While posting has proven to be valuable tool for me to digest information so that it’s understood and remembered. The process of posting has been problematic. So I had to do changes.

entry.title: Asking Too Much
entry.content:
I’ve been following government responses on cyber and infosec incidents and internet mass surveillance for years now. I even wrote few starry-eyed posts asking for ownership. This year, however, I got educated: Perhaps I’m asking too much.

From the user’s perspective only real visibile cue that something is happening on the regulation front are the prompts asking for accepting cookies when you visit web sites. Frankly, that does nothing to bring control over mass surveillance economy that is the defacto business model for most significant web companies.

So, if this is the best tangible result after years and rounds of international legislation, it’s no wonder why bigger global economies have developed or are in process of developing their own social media platforms, for instance. Obviously this topic of geo-platforms is more complicated than just that.

The Finnish Vastaamo case is an oustanding example. If you rule out the normal internal responses of firing the executive(s) in charge, nothing has happened. The lack of public response from any authority is mindboggling. Simultaneously, individual users are being extorted to regain their data gathered in private therapy sessions.

The Garmin case further proved the point that you can be breached and potentially lose very sensitive health and position data without being held accountable. Garmin provides services in Europe and according to their privacy policy adhere to GDPR - and most likely many other privacy laws globally.

Therefore they should’ve notified their users and explained whether any data was lost. At the time of writing, I did many searches and didn’t find any public statement of the impact to the end user’s privacy. Garmin ended up paying the ransom but it’s still unknown how they improved their security after the incident and how they got hacked.

No significant improvements are in horizon for 2021. Sadly, it’s the opposite. We should expect new incidents reported with similar narratives outlined in this post.
entry.summary: I’ve been following government responses on cyber and infosec incidents and internet mass surveillance for years now. I even wrote few starry-eyed posts asking for ownership. This year, however, I got educated: Perhaps I’m asking too much.

entry.title: On Planned Maintenance Work
entry.content:
Some days ago, I noticed emerging news items covering major maintenance windows of Finnish banks OP and Nordea reported by YLE (Finnish Broadcasting Company). Because of Corona pandemia this leaves consumers in a tricky situation since cash payments are not accepted broadly at the moment, according to the article. Hence YLE reported the planned maintenance in the news.

A couple of vantage points on this:

  • Consumers and businesses should be always prepared for situations where electronic banking is not available. This means planning that looks beyond tomorrow. Nothing more to say about this.
  • Maintenance work should not be a surprise to consumers. Instead, it should be a sign of operational quality and it should be expected. The lack of it is an alarming sign. Even keeping the system “as-is” means periodic maintenance. Without maintenance any system will start to show signs of degradation within 6-12 months, based on personal experience. You should not trust systems that are not maintained continually. Especially on financial matters.
  • Since companies have largely moved to work from home policies globally the attack surface is larger than ever. In fact, it has grown exponentially in few months. We’re waiting to see that exploited. That’s why keeping systems safe is extremely important right now.
  • From operational perspective many maintenance activities can be automated. Automation here helps to reduce the length and the cost of the maintenance window. Therefore decreasing the impact on the consumer. However, automated workflows haven’t been implemented often and many orgs are forced to labor through complex changes relying on manual work.

That’s it for now. Make sure services and systems you use are maintained often. Sometimes that means accepting assets not being available. This shouldn’t be a problem unless you have neglected contingency planning.

Finally, remember that the good guys are doing thankless jobs while you’re sleeping and kick off their normal duty in the morning breaking their normal sleep cycle. They do this to protect you and your business.
entry.summary: Some days ago, I noticed emerging news items covering major maintenance windows of Finnish banks OP and Nordea reported by YLE (Finnish Broadcasting Company). Because of Corona pandemia this leaves consumers in a tricky situation since cash payments are not accepted broadly at the moment, according to the article. Hence YLE reported the planned maintenance in the news.

entry.title: Agile Manifesto Reviewed
entry.content:
I seem to read Agile Manifesto periodically. It has to be because different discussions and trainings point you back to review the original manifesto. Although it’s starting to be quite dated, I can understand this ask. Agile has become an important product development method which touches most IT projects today and sometimes dominates the discussion. So instead of just reading it through (again), let’s review it and see how it holds its ground today.

The Balance Between Value and Devalue

Almost everything we do is a balancing act between extremities - and the winner often is the most balanced individual and organization. So when reflecting that viewpoint on the manifesto, I’ve started to read it as a frustrated but polite bottom to up engineering rebellion. This may take some lines to explain.

The extremity that the manifesto devalues (see picture above) is frustrating to any project team because it’s often out of their control and stand in the way of progress. Or at least slows it down.

If you want to build something and are working directly on a customer interface, it’s logical that you desire to remove all these speed bumps. You want to concentrate building your product as well and fast you can. This is how you’re recognized as a developer. I get that. I know how liberating it is to concentrate on a technical problem without disturbances.

Here’s where this gets challenging: Although the manifesto says that it has “come to value” one extremity over another, I argue that not many reads it exactly. I think - rather fear that some reads it so that you can let the other extremity overrun the other one. This on its own is an alarming mindset.

I think the manifesto just tries to make a point that you should put more emphasis on valued extremity and reduce costs on devalued extremity. For instance, let’s add more 10% testing hours and reduce the same amount from non-critical documentation or we can afford 8% overdelivery if we avoid contract re-negotiation.

Most propably there is not a winning pattern of how to make a time or cost split between the extremities. It’s complicated because organizations, projects and teams are different. Some teams could be happily overcommited to documentation due to legit regulation reasons but some could’ve just developed a dislike to it since it takes their attention away from engineering.

Leadership and management skills are needed to strike the balance. If there’s a vacuum of that and there are no established policies, no wonder people are manifesting in protest to devalue the extremity that is not their duty anyways. However, I argue that in long-term you can’t devalue, e.g., proper process and communications and run a business that is handled professionally.

Jokes work because they capture the reality in a fun and often painful manner. So Google around and you find ample of jokes about Agile and implemeting it in a too extreme manner.

I recognize setting the balance on the “valued-vs-devalued axis” as a success criteria. Let’s back that up by reviewing the parts of the valued extreme below.

Sprints

Although sprints are a great vehicle for getting stuff done early and often, they don’t offer a long-term planning capability. So even if we are emphasizing sprints as a main delivery model, there should be people looking from outside in so that sprint teams are actually accomplishing objectives that not only tackle today’s priorities but are aligned to long-term goals.

Looking from the architecture perspective, I don’t fully support the idea either that documentation activies are devalued. If you have a long-term goal, you should know where you stand to get there. Without updated documentation understanding the gap is impossible. “That guy knows” is not a proper method of knowledge management. Information should be decentralized since Agile expects decentralized actions.

Therefore, in my opinion, sprints need to produce clear documentation as an outcome. Do we need to spend days and weeks to fine tune massive Word documents? No! But we need to find a way that provides the documentation capability that fills our requirements on the content and reduces time spend on it.

Could we document as-built or Infrastructure as Code appended with some great diagrams? Absolutely! As long as it gets done and you are able to reduce costs included to it.

Because sprints require experience, speed and decision-making capability from all individuals so only staff that is trained properly should participate. Sprints are not necessarily the right place for learning on the job.

Finally, you don’t always get the most fired up and self-driven people either. Which is fine. We all are different. However, just note that the manifesto and principles that come along are somewhat idealistic about this. In real life, you take what you have and make the most out of it.

This is where this engineering driven manifesto runs under the hem of the management. Sprints require heightened attention and engagement from the managent, in my opinion.

“Plans are useless, but planning is indispensable”

The idea of working in a collaborative manner rather that concentrating on contract negotiation must come from the fact that Scrum has been designed to tackle complex problems. Problems that are not even clearly postulated until you start working on them. If they would be simple they would fall under well-established processes.

That’s why plans often fail on the face of complexity. Planning on the other hand is extremely important. I don’t think Manifesto’s “responding to change over following a plan” is good guidance. Experience shows it should be more like “adjust your planning as the situation unfolds”.

You should try to be aggressive rather reactive which means you adjust your planning and make micro decisions as the situation unfolds. Planning helps to identify the need of reaction, gives you reaction options and gives you time to think before the reaction must be decided. This general principle applies to almost everything we do.

Individuals Over Processes

Without processes we would simply be as good as we are as individuals. So if something works really well, it should be documented on high level and deployed in the organization. This way it becomes a standard and a measurable reputation of the organization.

On the other hand, individuals should have decision making capability so that decisions can be made as close to action as possible. The main challenge is that you have to make sure people can differentiate a good decision from a bad decision. This can be helped with training and experience but still at best depends on the individual.

That’s why, I believe having a high-level process that allows local decision-making may be producing the best performance at the time of writing. Process should be known and there may be some well-known procedures but you should know what you can change and how much. The process should be further developed (ideally) while the product is developed. There should be same motivation to improve both.

Certainly we shouldn’t try to put individual decision making compete against processess because they are what we have in terms of organization performance. They both are factors in the same equation.

Conclusions

The original manifesto is already old (2001). To set the intent better it should be reviewed and renewed. Clearly, even though the industry is still shifting, lessons learned have been made. It should be pointed out that the 12 principles give more guidance on the objectives, but like the original manifesto, they demand balancing to avoid poor results.

When it comes to writing manifestos in the future, I think good relationships in the chain of command beats them. Writing a paper and calling it manifesto reads like a peaceful mutiny which alone should be a sign that something is not right.

We learn as we go.
entry.summary: I seem to read Agile Manifesto periodically. It has to be because different discussions and trainings point you back to review the original manifesto. Although it’s starting to be quite dated, I can understand this ask. Agile has become an important product development method which touches most IT projects today and sometimes dominates the discussion. So instead of just reading it through (again), let’s review it and see how it holds its ground today.

entry.title: Feed DDoS
entry.content:
I’ve been following up the social media ever since the protests started in the US. To set it off: My very limited understanding of the situation and history, doesn’t provide me to have a public opinion on it. I’m just making observations on the technology that seems to lay the foundation for messaging and sharing information during an incident like this.

That all said, I noticed a few days back that my feeds started filling from single colored screens that, to my limited understanding, were posted to show support and express solidarity. Psychologist could probably explain how group thinking and safety concepts work in these types of situations. However even as a layman it is safe to assume that expressing opinion, let the reason be any, is important. Luckily we have social media that allows to broadcast one’s viewpoint.

The flip side of social media becomes problematic from an engineering perspective - which just might be the worst viewpoint at the moment. Few days back I woke up, checked my feeds and I couldn’t help thinking that either my feeds were DDoS‘d or the platform was hacked. For I was browsing for news and information how the situation had evolved over night and all I saw were single colored squares on the feed. Feed DDoS is just like any other DDoS but instead of clogging the network pipes it glogs the information feed very visibly on application layer.

Although expressing support to the people is important, it doesn’t come without a cost. The cost in my case was that I couldn’t get signifcant updates of the situation anymore. That in turn rendered social media’s main capabilties useless. We should be cognizant of that cost.

To manage the cost of relying on social media during incidents we as a countries and individuals should try to find answers to the following questions at minimum:

  • How much trust can be safely put on social media during incidents?
  • How much of the new content provides updated information on the situation?
  • Who are the players that are actively shaping our feeds?
  • Are some of them our adversaries?
  • How do we validate information offered to us?

When there is DDoS in the networks the first response is to get it under control and restore the service. We should try to develop methods to maintain voluntary or involuntary Feed DDoSs so that the important and validated information is still raised to our attention. This incident has made it clear that there is much to do on that front.
entry.summary: I’ve been following up the social media ever since the protests started in the US. To set it off: My very limited understanding of the situation and history, doesn’t provide me to have a public opinion on it. I’m just making observations on the technology that seems to lay the foundation for messaging and sharing information during an incident like this.

entry.title: On Zero Trust Networking (Briefly)
entry.content:
Zero Trust Networking (ZTN) is one of the main trends in the security minded networking industry today. While many have a tremendous appetite to implement ZTN capabilty only few are able to. Why?

To implement ZTN, you have to have an outstanding understanding on your enterprise applications. Understanding how the applications is and should be allowed to communicate with its own tiers and peer applications becomes a must.

In a nutshell, ZTN implements a logical firewall between every application tier and application. That’s why ZTN must apply rules or contracts to allow only the bare minimum of the traffic. The traffic that is designed to be there and nothing else. Easier said than done when figuring out how tight rule set you should operate.

For example, Cisco ACI implements ZTN using contracts (image source)

The solution for this is to run periodic application dependency mappings or have a tool that visualizes the current application flows in real time. This mitigates the main problem of running hundreds of applications that form up a massive, invisible web of dependencies. Not understanding what applications do and how tight control we should implement is the biggest obstacle for ZTN approach (based on my own experience).

Just remember even having 20/20 visibility to your application dependencies doesn’t allow you to transform your production network to ZTN over night. However, it gives you your current situation which you can you consume to prioritize, plan and move your network to ZTN one application or application group at a time.

The battle against “permit any any” has begun.
entry.summary: Zero Trust Networking (ZTN) is one of the main trends in the security minded networking industry today. While many have a tremendous appetite to implement ZTN capabilty only few are able to. Why?

entry.title: The Most Important Cyber Attacks Thus Far

link.title: The Most Important Cyber Attacks Thus Far

entry.content:
Atlanta Ransomware Incident 2018

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2018_Atlanta_cyberattack

This incident showed the monetary impact a cyber attack can have on a community.
City spent roughly 17 million USD to recover and remedy their services. That
price tag keeps increasing according to press due to ongoing remediation
efforts, which naturally does not compensate the data destroyed
during the incident. Some newspapers have cited a GDP loss of 3 billion USD -
quite a remarkable loss.

A modern city in one of the most advanced countries on the world put on its
knees by two Iranian hackers. Needless to say, it makes you think hard how can
this happen.

It turns out Atlanta showed weakness in the area that is quite common to the
whole networking industry: aged infrastructure that was not patched properly.
Agreed, sometimes it is hard to keep up with the fast upgrade cycle, but this
does not justify complacency. Aged software and hardware keeps on being the one
of the main reasons why security of computer networks is risked on all aspects
of CIA triad.

Furthermore, it seems that Atlanta had weak or no password policy in place
which allowed attackers to use brute force attack against the systems. I’m sure
the research team found other weaknesses as well.

Combining poor life-cycle management with lack of password policy alone is,
in my opinion, a sufficient evidence of not having a credible security program
in place. If cities of this size and development are not ready to defend
themselves, what about the smaller cities and countries of less wealth.
This is why this incident still gives me the creeps. Atlanta was just
the first of many.

Cyber campaign in Ukraine 2015-present

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/December_2015_Ukraine_power_grid_cyberattack
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2017_cyberattacks_on_Ukraine
https://blog.talosintelligence.com/2018/05/VPNFilter.html
https://www.politico.eu/article/ukraine-cyber-war-frontline-russia-malware-attacks/
https://www.ft.com/content/8db7251c-1411-11e9-a581-4ff78404524e

The annexation of Crimea conducted by Russia demonstrated a well-orchestrated
combination of kinetic operations and cyber warfare. Now, it could be that the
annexation and the cyber incidents in Ukraine happened coincidently at the same
time because of the country’s weakened political posture.

However, I am having a tremendous problem swallowing that. Regardless,
the combination of the incidents that took place between 2015 and now
demonstrate an important shift on how modern societies can be disrupted via
cyber attacks. I cannot recall any other country that has been publicly reported
being under cyber attacks for such a long period of time. Ukraine has been a
cyber weapons range for the past five years and no sanctions have been
handed out. It’s scary that cyberspace is a wild west if the actor is suspected
to be a nation state. Imagine going in IT or any business in Ukraine. You
basically can’t because your information is ok’ed for stealing, business
operation ok’ed to be disrupted and no cyber insurance will cover your losses
(ask Mondelez for instance)

In effect, this cyber warfare campaign against Ukraine has made it a no-go-zone
for modern businesses. The problems have moved from cyber space to kinetic space
and these attacks have become very real for their victims. Cyber attacks can’t
be treated as a technical problems anymore.

Mirai aka The Fall of the Internet 2016

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mirai_(malware)

Although not an attack to target a single victim, Mirai targeted a specific
technology. Mirai was the first large attack focused on IoT. Prior to Mirai
there had been Internet outages due to BGP misconfiguration, submarine
cable disruptions and, surely, attacks against services such as DNS. Why Mirai
was an eye opener to me is because it painted a picture of the future ahead of
us. Almost everything is networked. In few years, we are not going to even use
the expression such as “I will connect it to Internet”. We are connected the
same way electricity is connected to a fridge: it just is.

Mirai attacked that early on. It did the same thing for IoT as Stuxnet did for
industrial networks, it exploited the fact that security was not a even an
after-thought for these products. IoT devices as opposed to typical network
devices are not designed or procured or even operated by people that have an
understanding of securing connected environments. Therefore, it was easy for
Mirai to exploit the default settings these devices came configured with.

It’s worrying since majority of procurement departments are unsure how much they
should pay for security and how much is the right amount of security. If there
is no interest to pay of security features and hardened configuration baselines,
there won’t be those by default. Our industry must take great measures in
changing attitudes how products are designed and deployed before incidents such
as Mirai become a rarity.

On the other hand, Mirai grandly showcased the great weakenesses of Internet.
What started as a “never-go-down” network has become relic of the past that
still relies on insecure DNS and BGP protocols. I know, I’m pushing here a l
ittle bit, but I think that’s ok since there has been only incremental
development to Internet’s core technology since the foundation was established.
Of the protocols BGP is out of Mirai’s scope but DNS is not. Put some pressure
on DNS providers (e.g. Dyn) using DDoS and suddendly you have a large Internet
outage at hand.

To sum it up connecting any computing platform to any network with a non-harde-
ned configuration baseline is a cardinal mistake. Make the same mistake too many
times and we’ll have a new Mirai-like incident at hand.

Value chain breaches back in the day and now

http://edition.cnn.com/TECH/computing/9909/03/windows.nsa.02/
https://www.ccleaner.com/news/blog/2017/9/18/security-notification-for-ccleaner-v5336162-and-ccleaner-cloud-v1073191-for-32-bit-windows-users

I remember the day well when _NSAKEY hit the news. I was on the seventh
grade (‘99). One of my buddies came to school all anxious explaining an NSA
backdoor had been installed to Windows 95 (It was a maths class and we all were
into computers. It’s ok to chuckle.). I knew little back then so I had to
check the terms backdoor and NSA but as soon as I had ran my Altavista(?)
searches, I understood not to trust operating systems, implicitly, ever again.

Well-known brands come with some level of in-built trust. As an enterprise or a
consumer you would like to think that when something is widely adopted it has to
be good enough to trust implicitly. Value chain attacks exploit this weakness.
While the perimeter protection on network edges has become good enough in
general, the next consideration is to find a way to infiltrate malicious code
in the computers and networks through some other delivery channel instead of
using network connections. Enter the value chain breaches.

CCleaner in 2017 was a very unfortunate case example of a value chain breach
completed succesfully by the adversary. In this case the malicous code was
inserted “at the factory” making it almost impossible for the victims to realize
they were under attack. Even more so when the data exfiltration from the net
seemed totally legitimate. Ever since this has been one of the major threats
big technology companies are focusing on their SDLC program - how to make sure
someone has not tampered with the original code or the update patches.

I listed this incident here since it hit so many organization in the teeth.
Trust is earned over years but lost in a single heartbeat. Makes you think
how long it will take CCleaner to re-establish that trust. For some companies
this would mean they are out of business for good. Without security there is no
business to operate.

entry.title: Application Level Ecryption Is Not Enough - Alone

link.title: Application Level Ecryption Is Not Enough - Alone

entry.content:
It’s pretty common that clients ask about the role of network encryption (VPNs, MACSec etc.) now that almost all web applications utilize TLS encryption. The short blanket answer is: absolutely needed. Although there are plenty of reasons supporting that opinion, I think the picture below should help you to make up your mind on this topic.

MUSCULAR (DS-200B) surveillance program,
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MUSCULAR_(surveillance_program)

Few years ago, a surveillance program called Muscular exploited a design flaw in cloud applications in which only the traffic to web front-end was encrypted. The traffic within the data centers was in clear text. Taking into account the geographical dispersion of the data centers this made/makes the interception of the traffic more than possible for the interested party.

This is troubling since most enterprises have on-boarded cloud based workplace applications. The case is a reminder that you shouldn’t expect that your provider has implemented comprehensive encryption by default. This was the caveat that Muscular exploited. Therefore, it’s important to know how your provider has secured their production networks against attacks on confidentiality.

Similar outcome can be a result of operating hundreds of private applications that are of different generations. In this case, the problem lies in varying design requirements, implementation and the sheer volume. That’s why it may be extremely hard or impossible to assure fully encrypted application layer even if you are operating your own applications. Typical examples include:

  • Web front ends deployed with vulnerable versions of SSL or
  • One tier of the three tier application doesn’t provide any kind of encryption or
  • Like in the case above, there is no network level encryption between production sites.

Facts on the table, it is hard not to consider network level encryption technologies like MACSec and VPNs as an added layer of protection for the applications.
entry.summary: It’s pretty common that clients ask about the role of network encryption (VPNs, MACSec etc.) now that almost all web applications utilize TLS encryption. The short blanket answer is: absolutely needed. Although there are plenty of reasons supporting that opinion, I think the picture below should help you to make up your mind on this topic.

entry.title: Pentest Setup with Docker
entry.content:
Few months back I took a personal challenge to use containers whenever it seemed to fit the purpose. While there were many reasons to do this, one stood taller than the others: portability. I typically work on software using different HW platforms: Windows or Linux PC at work, Mac or Raspberry at home. By using Docker containers I was able to continue and edit my work from all of these platforms without wasting time on tool/module installation and setup.

I wanted to re-build my penetration testing setup with the same principles in mind (ethical hacking studies, if you wonder). I wanted to standardize the setup and make the installation as automatic as possible between different devices. I thought this would help me to stay focused on the actual task rather than honing and troubleshooting my toolkit. In summary: develop once and consume everywhere.

I started by pulling Kali docker image. The official image is just the beginning since it comes as a quite minimalist setup. For me it was a good idea to continue by installing Metasploit, kali-linux-top10 and few system tools that didn’t come as part of the base image.

If you’re following the same steps, after installing the first batch of tools it’s a really good idea to “save” your container. So that when you stop it you won’t need to install and configure the system again. In Docker’s terms you need to commit the container that you created and modified. This CLI command makes sure that your custom container is available right away when you need to re-launch it or move it on a different host. Just to emphasize: Moving tools around between different hosts was and is my main requirement - that’s why I like Docker and Python.

That was it for the attack suite (for now, more to do in near future). Next step: targets. In this context, I typically like to use Metasploitable and Vagrant. So, I immediately started browsing for a container version. However, there seems not to be a version that can run on Docker at the moment.

This is how I found DVWA - a Damn Vulnerable Web Application. Since it is an application, it doesn’t offer OS level vulnerabilities. However, it is a good starting point as any. I need to look into how to build a target that offers more than just the application level vulnerabilities. There’s a vague idea, but it needs more investigation.

Container networking is built so that the attacker and victim can only talk to one another, which in my opinion, is always a good precaution to keep things under control. Just to point out: DVWA shouldn’t be exposed to external networks. As the name suggests, it is damn vulnerable as you probably saw from the screen capture above.

Building this setup didn’t take more than few hours counting in positive interruptions like my son pooping his diapers or cooking dinner. Now the next step is to start writing exploits on Python and consuming Metasploit modules.

Pointer for the like-minded: the login page seem to accept username and password combination accompanied by a CSRF token. Interesting..
entry.summary: Few months back I took a personal challenge to use containers whenever it seemed to fit the purpose. While there were many reasons to do this, one stood taller than the others: portability. I typically work on software using different HW platforms: Windows or Linux PC at work, Mac or Raspberry at home. By using Docker containers I was able to continue and edit my work from all of these platforms without wasting time on tool/module installation and setup.

entry.title: Decision Making for Architecture Design
entry.content:
Procrastination is a common problem in architecture development initiatives. In fact, it’s a problem that dictates almost every move we make. When things are complicated it is easy to be overwhelmed and hard to get started. At the same time, no-one argues about the importance of the informed first move which gives the momentum that builds on itself.

Procrastinating (and missing) that first step is unacceptable, since most contemporary organizations born and die because of technology. In this post, I’ll recommend remedies for this problem by sharing lessons learned on prototyping, land navigation, leading an infantry platoon and MMA. Yeah, I KNOW! It sounds pretty random, but it turns out to be surprisingly important.

First, let’s establish key terminology: Architecture design is a program and a deliverable. It’s a program that is long and often without a clear end date. The deliverable side of architecture covers a long-term technological vision aimed to put complex goals and requirements in an easily understandable format. The requirements change over the time which is known as “trying to hit a moving target”. These combined create two large gaps between intention and architecture, and architecture and implementation.

That’s why, in my opinion, making decisions get complicated. The following principles have proven useful to keep things squared away:

ATOMIC DECISIONS

People who don’t have experience in fighting often think that an MMA fight is a flow of primal instinct. This may be the case sometimes, but I can assure you that it is not a long-term winning tactic. When you are looking your opponent eye to eye, your mind goes to overdrive. You ears are ringing from the excitement caused by adrenaline, fear and the need to act. And while it does not feel like you’re thinking, you are capable of decisions making. T

he difference is that those decisions, even responses have been made before you ever stepped on the mat. When you are rolling and practicing you have the opportunity to pause and build those rehearsed action plans. If you don’t do that you are already lost. As an example: what if the opponent throws a right hand, shall you duck and counter or shall you shoot for the legs. It should be easy to decide on the fly, right? Wrong:

  • You have to choose from the tens of techniques that you know,
  • You need to consider the counters the opponent can do,
  • You need to think of how much energy you are ready to consume,
  • You have to manage your fears and
  • You have to assess the risk of losing after your move.

It’s too much to handle. Therefore, don’t make decisions, especially the big ones, real-time. However, make small “atomic” risk-free decisions often. If possible, analyze those decisions prior the need to decide. You can faint, jab or move quite risk free and while improving your position. Feedback on these smaller decisions, even failing few of them, prepare you to make the bigger decisions that hit the target. This way you can take the hard first step without risking investing too much to it.

Sometimes when you are in the woods and don’t know exactly where, it helps to move a bit to find yourself on a map. Maybe you see a lake, a hill or a road that puts you back on the map. I find this also true when I’m in a new city relying on GPS. It does not matter if your first small move moves you away from your destination since now you know which direction your compass needle is pointing. Make small decisions and adjust when you see the result. This way the big risky decisions become manageable.

What was explained is the concept but before you can make atomic decisions you have to understand what is important, what are the decisions and what is the substance you’re considering. Let’s build that workflow next.

SET THE AGENDA, PRIORITIZE AND SET BACK STOPS

First step of building atomic decision making is to determine what is chronologically important. Consider this simple example: Let’s assume you should create a secure network design for a global company. You start by setting the agenda, for example, it should be a site to site VPN, centrally managed to drive down operational expenditure and follow ISO control framework to make sure the solution is credible from the get go.

Alternatively, if you find yourself raving about topics that people around you find hard to justify, there is a high risk that you are prioritizing the wrong decisions. Example red flags in this context typically are what devices are to be bought, how are you going to implement IKEv2, if that new BGP bug is going to crash the entire solution or how to deal with that one site’s salty admin.

Lack of prioritization is one of the main reasons why it is hard to start making progress on architecture development. Often this materializes on two observations: trying to commit to too big and complex decisions too soon or wrong decisions prioritized on the wrong moment (e.g. diving into implementation details directly). Here your land navigation skills come to play once more.

Ever heard of back stops? Every single time we prepared maps in Scouts or in the army we marked it with well-known back stops. Back stops are significant terrain features that tell you, you’ve gone too far from your original objective.

Even though people wiser than me had told me to use back stops ever since I was a boy, I learned the importance of them after leading my rifle platoon in the middle of a swamp on a cold, damp, dark autumn night. I still see the 35 annoyed wet, tired and dirty faces looking at me with utmost disgust and disappointment. Upon this humbling experience, I could just take my lumps and learn from them.

After that I marked my maps with one to three back stops for an objective. You need secondary and tertiary back stops to stop you from going if you’re way off the target. When you hit a back stop, you stop and try to get back to the original objective. Some years ago, I realized that you could and should use back stops in design work. Few examples below:

  • Don’t go planning physical work or designing routing if the business and functional requirements are not documented,
  • Don’t start specify security features if you don’t recognize the law or control framework that should be followed,
  • If you are already choosing tools, but can’t articulate well why they are needed,
  • If the discussion is around hype words like digitization, machine learning or IoT, but no-one knows how to make money from it,
  • And so on.

Take a pause to create a timeline of your high level priorities and make sure to implement back stops that alert you in case you are prioritizing wrong decisions or if you are going too deep too early. Once you have an understanding of the priorities your next steps are to divide those to atomic decisions and to understand the content of the decision.

EXPOSE THE ATOMIC DECISIONS

While prioritization is a common leadership practice, even some of the most know publications stop after that recommendation. It’s common sense to do the first thing first. Instead the problem is that you don’t know what to prioritize before you have a list of decisions to be made in front of you. To make such a list you need a hell lot of a knowledge and experience to understand how to divide your priorities to meaningful atomic decisions.

On the other hand, if you aren’t able to do this you will go suggesting risky solutions and performing difficult projects and end up re-actively being notified of the decisions you should make. To go back to my MMA example this translate to as if you would try to make a decision analysis while 100 kg opponent grinds your face on the mat and tries to take your arm home with him. In short, you are too late and about to lose. Aim to think and live the decisions before taking them.

Sounds academic? It’s not. If you are inclined to business and leadership, it means you need to read case studies to learn what people before you did to solve similar problems. If you are inclined to technology, you must put in some serious lab time to understand the key decisions. These exercises help you to create the list of decisions, to rehearse most decisions in a “free-to-fail” setup and gives you time to concentrate on the ones that you didn’t know to expect.

I just recently worked on a very demanding complex use case. The problem was that I wasn’t sure if we could do it. At the same time, this use case played a significant role in the sprint’s value proposition. To avoid losing face and leading our sprint team to a cold, freezing, damp swamp of misery, I wanted to divide the decision and the faith of this use case to multiple atomic decisions to be done in a priority order. This is how it looked like:

  • We did a technical feasibility analysis for the use case, after which we decided on the potential implementations
  • I drew two primary candidates how the use case could be completed and two contingency options that partially filled customer requirements
  • We analyzed those again based on customer requirements and selected the primary option and one as a contingency option
  • We built a small lab for the primary option, to get more confidence
  • After labbing we decided to commit the primary option to the sprint
  • In addition, we decided to keep the contingency plan in place, should something go wrong in the sprint

This is how atomic decisions could look like. Take small early commitments towards the grand decision. On each step we learned more about the use case’s complexity which put us on a much better position to maneuver as the challenges were revealed. Blindly committing this use case to the sprint would’ve been a major failure from my part.

KNOW THE DECISION

There seems to be a myth out on the wild that architects don’t need to know how to actually do things, they just need to be able to speak the “right language”. I’ll have to call this for bullshit. In general, following this advice is a very bad idea. The more you know, the better you are able to sanity check your decisions. It is as simple as that.

The army knows this the best. Me and the brothers before me until the dawn of time, who went through the platoon leader training, were taught every single job in the platoon. Platoon leader’s job is not to shoot the machine gun, but he must understand how the system works, has experience shooting it, know how to maintain it, how to position it for the best firepower and how to attack against it. This is the caveat: you won’t be the best expert on it, but you still can operate it if needed.

To divide your priorities to atomic goals and to make meaningful sanity checks on them you must be able to prototype your solution. Do you need to be able to build it to a fully operating production system? Not always but sometimes yes!

Without knowledge, it’s impossible to determine what decisions there may be ahead, where to start your journey and how likely your priorities can be completed. I argue that not having enough knowledge is one of the prime reasons why there is so much delay between design and implementation.

Gaining knowledge is easy: sit down, read, think, listen to critique from your peers and build prototypes - for example: in order to better understand design principles of Docker and Kubernetes, I built a swarm on a couple of Raspberry Pis that host a self-made, simple two-tier web application. Gaining knowledge shares the same misconception as working out. You don’t need fancy equipment or specialized program to be in shape. What you need is to feel sweat blinding your eyes, your lungs begging for air and iron tearing holes in your palms.

CONCLUSIONS

So there it is, make small decisions often and only after due assurance commit to bigger decisions, set your timeline of priorities, create back stops, understand what decisions you should make before you are in a losing position, study cases, put in time in the lab and know how to do every job in the shop but don’t try to pose as the best expert.

Although the list is not complete and I’m not a complete advisor on this field, I can guarantee a positive effect in your projects if you embrace some the lessons learned discussed in this post.

Don’t be afraid to struggle.
entry.summary: Procrastination is a common problem in architecture development initiatives. In fact, it’s a problem that dictates almost every move we make. When things are complicated it is easy to be overwhelmed and hard to get started. At the same time, no-one argues about the importance of the informed first move which gives the momentum that builds on itself.
